definition: Airway opening technique lifting chin while tilting head back
rationale: Head-tilt chin-lift opens the airway by extending the neck and lifting the chin which pulls the tongue away from the posterior pharynx. It is contraindicated in suspected spinal injury.

definition: Airway technique lifting mandible without moving neck
rationale: The jaw-thrust maneuver lifts the mandible forward opening the airway without neck extension. It is the preferred technique for trauma patients with suspected cervical spine injury.

definition: Side-lying position to protect airway in unconscious breathing patient
rationale: The recovery position places an unconscious breathing patient on their side allowing fluids to drain from the mouth and preventing tongue obstruction. It is used when no spinal injury is suspected.

definition: Removing fluids and secretions from the airway
rationale: Suctioning uses negative pressure to remove blood vomit and secretions from the airway. Duration is limited to 10-15 seconds to prevent hypoxia. Suction only on withdrawal of the catheter.

definition: Placing oropharyngeal airway to maintain patent airway
rationale: OPA insertion involves sizing the airway measuring from corner of mouth to earlobe and inserting with tip toward palate then rotating 180 degrees or inserting sideways and rotating. It requires absent gag reflex.

definition: Placing nasopharyngeal airway through the nostril
rationale: NPA insertion involves sizing from nostril to earlobe lubricating and inserting along the floor of the nasal cavity with bevel toward septum. It is better tolerated than OPA in patients with intact gag reflex.

definition: Delivering positive pressure breaths using BVM device
rationale: BVM ventilation provides positive pressure breaths by squeezing the bag while maintaining mask seal. Two-person technique improves seal and ventilation. Ventilate over 1 second watching for chest rise.

definition: Rescue breathing through barrier mask device
rationale: Mouth-to-mask ventilation uses a pocket mask as a barrier while providing rescue breaths. The mask has a one-way valve and can connect to oxygen. It is used when BVM is unavailable.

definition: Providing supplemental oxygen to increase inspired oxygen
rationale: Oxygen administration involves selecting appropriate delivery device based on patient needs attaching to oxygen source setting flow rate and applying to patient. Monitor SpO2 and adjust as needed.

definition: Rhythmic pressing on sternum to circulate blood during cardiac arrest
rationale: Chest compressions manually pump the heart to circulate blood during cardiac arrest. Push hard at least 2 inches at 100-120/minute allowing full recoil. Minimize interruptions.

definition: Delivering electrical shock to restore normal heart rhythm
rationale: Defibrillation delivers an electrical shock to depolarize the heart and allow normal rhythm to resume. AEDs analyze rhythm and advise shock for VF and pulseless VT only.

definition: Using automated defibrillator to analyze rhythm and deliver shocks
rationale: AED operation involves powering on attaching pads analyzing rhythm clearing the patient and delivering shock when advised. Resume CPR immediately after shock.

definition: Palpating artery to assess for heartbeat
rationale: Pulse check involves palpating an artery to detect heartbeat. Check carotid in adults brachial in infants. Limit pulse checks to 10 seconds during CPR to minimize interruptions.

definition: Applying firm pressure to wound to control bleeding
rationale: Direct pressure is the first-line technique for bleeding control. Apply firm continuous pressure with gloved hand and sterile dressing. Do not release to check if bleeding has stopped.

definition: Placing constricting band to stop severe extremity bleeding
rationale: Tourniquet application involves placing the device 2-3 inches above the wound and tightening until bleeding stops completely. Note the time of application. Do not remove once placed.

definition: Filling wound cavity with gauze to control bleeding
rationale: Wound packing involves firmly pressing gauze deep into a wound cavity to apply pressure from within. It is used for junctional wounds that cannot be tourniqueted such as groin armpit and neck.

definition: Initial rapid evaluation for life-threatening conditions
rationale: Primary assessment rapidly identifies and treats immediate life threats following ABCDE sequence. It takes only 60-90 seconds and drives immediate interventions before detailed assessment.

definition: Detailed head-to-toe physical examination
rationale: Secondary assessment is the systematic detailed examination performed after life threats are addressed. It includes head-to-toe exam vital signs and SAMPLE history to identify all problems.

definition: Obtaining pulse blood pressure respirations and other measurements
rationale: Vital signs measurement quantifies patient status including heart rate blood pressure respiratory rate temperature and SpO2. Serial measurements allow trending to identify improvement or deterioration.

definition: Measuring blood sugar level from finger stick sample
rationale: Blood glucose testing involves obtaining a small blood sample usually from a finger stick and using a glucometer to measure glucose concentration. It helps identify hypo or hyperglycemia.

definition: Listening to body sounds with stethoscope
rationale: Auscultation uses a stethoscope to listen to lung sounds heart sounds bowel sounds and blood pressure. It requires a quiet environment and skill to interpret findings.

definition: Examining by touch to assess various findings
rationale: Palpation uses touch to assess pulses skin condition tenderness deformity crepitus and other findings. Use appropriate pressure and observe patient response for pain.

definition: Holding head and neck in neutral alignment by hand
rationale: Manual inline stabilization involves holding the patient's head in neutral position to prevent cervical spine movement. It is maintained continuously until the patient is fully immobilized.

definition: Placing neck immobilization device on patient
rationale: C-collar application involves measuring for proper size then applying the collar while maintaining manual stabilization. The collar alone does not provide complete immobilization.

definition: Technique for turning patient while maintaining spinal alignment
rationale: The log roll turns a patient as a unit maintaining spinal alignment. One rescuer maintains head stabilization while others roll the body together. It is used for examination and backboard placement.

definition: Securing patient to rigid board for spinal protection
rationale: Backboard immobilization secures the patient to a rigid board using straps and head immobilization devices. It prevents spinal movement during transport. Pad voids to prevent movement.

definition: Immobilizing injured extremity to prevent movement
rationale: Splinting immobilizes fractures and dislocations to prevent further injury reduce pain and protect neurovascular structures. Splint in position found unless pulse is absent. Check neurovascular status before and after.

definition: Applying pulling force to realign femur fracture
rationale: Traction splinting applies longitudinal pull to the leg for femur fractures reducing muscle spasm bleeding and pain. Contraindications include hip knee or lower leg injury.

definition: Giving sugar gel or liquid to hypoglycemic patient
rationale: Oral glucose administration involves placing glucose gel between cheek and gum or giving glucose liquid to a conscious hypoglycemic patient who can swallow. Never give to unconscious patients.

definition: Giving aspirin for suspected cardiac event
rationale: Aspirin administration for suspected MI involves having the patient chew 160-325mg of aspirin if not allergic and no active bleeding. Chewing provides faster absorption than swallowing whole.

definition: Helping patient take their prescribed nitroglycerin
rationale: Nitroglycerin assistance involves helping the patient use their own prescribed nitroglycerin for chest pain after confirming BP is adequate over 100 systolic. Place tablet or spray under tongue.

definition: Helping patient use their prescribed epinephrine
rationale: EpiPen assistance involves helping the patient or giving the patient's prescribed auto-injector for anaphylaxis. Remove safety press firmly into outer thigh hold 10 seconds and massage site.

definition: Helping patient use prescribed bronchodilator
rationale: Inhaler assistance involves helping patients use their prescribed MDI for respiratory distress. Shake the inhaler have patient exhale actuate while inhaling slowly and hold breath.

definition: Giving opioid reversal medication
rationale: Naloxone administration reverses opioid overdose effects. It can be given intranasally IM or IV depending on protocol. Be prepared for patient to become agitated or combative upon awakening.

definition: Techniques to stop bleeding
rationale: Hemorrhage control includes direct pressure wound packing tourniquets and hemostatic agents. The goal is to stop life-threatening bleeding as quickly as possible. Current guidelines emphasize early tourniquet use.

definition: Cleaning covering and protecting wounds
rationale: Wound care involves controlling bleeding cleaning contamination covering with appropriate dressings and protecting from further injury. Impaled objects are stabilized in place not removed.

definition: Emergency treatment of thermal chemical or electrical burns
rationale: Burn care involves stopping the burning process cooling removing non-adherent clothing and covering with clean dry dressings. Do not apply ice butter or ointments. Prevent hypothermia.

definition: Flushing eye with fluid to remove contaminants
rationale: Eye irrigation flushes contaminants from the eye using water or saline. For chemical exposure irrigate for at least 20 minutes from inner to outer corner. Hold eyelids open.

definition: Assisting childbirth in field when transport is not possible
rationale: Emergency delivery involves preparing for delivery supporting the baby as it emerges checking for cord around neck suctioning the newborn clamping and cutting the cord and caring for mother and baby.

definition: Emergency care for non-breathing or distressed newborn
rationale: Newborn resuscitation includes drying warming stimulating suctioning and providing ventilation and compressions if needed. Most newborns respond to basic stimulation and warming.

definition: Preparing patient for transport with appropriate immobilization
rationale: Patient packaging involves placing the patient on appropriate device backboard stretcher with proper immobilization protection from elements and securing for transport.

definition: Techniques for safely moving patients
rationale: Patient lifting requires proper body mechanics adequate personnel and appropriate equipment. Lift with legs keep load close communicate with partners and know limitations.

definition: Using wheeled cot for patient transport
rationale: Stretcher operation involves adjusting height loading patient properly securing restraints navigating terrain and loading into the ambulance. Two providers work together coordinating movements.

definition: Verbal patient information transmitted to receiving facility
rationale: Radio report provides the receiving hospital with patient information including age gender chief complaint vital signs treatments and ETA. It allows preparation for patient arrival.

definition: Transferring patient care to receiving facility
rationale: Patient handoff involves verbal report to receiving staff transferring monitoring documentation and patient belongings. Ensure all critical information is communicated directly.

definition: Written documentation of assessment and care provided
rationale: The PCR is the legal written record documenting the call including patient information assessment findings treatments patient response and transport information. It becomes part of the medical record.

definition: Recording patient's decision to decline care or transport
rationale: Refusal documentation must show the patient had capacity understood risks and still declined care. Include risks explained patient statements witnesses and signature. Protects provider and patient.

definition: Initial evaluation of scene for safety and resources
rationale: Scene size-up assesses scene safety number of patients mechanism or nature of illness and resource needs before approaching. It is the first step on every call and continues throughout.

definition: Using appropriate barriers for infection prevention
rationale: BSI or standard precautions involve using appropriate PPE for all patient contacts assuming all blood and body fluids are potentially infectious. Minimum is gloves with additional PPE as needed.

definition: Cleaning hands to prevent infection transmission
rationale: Hand hygiene is the single most important infection prevention measure. Wash with soap and water or use alcohol-based sanitizer before and after every patient contact even when gloves are worn.
